section.title: Divorce, Children, and the Rich Young Man
section.sectionRationale: On the road to Jerusalem Jesus teaches on three matters that test the heart: marriage, which He roots in God's creation design against a culture of easy divorce; children, whom He receives as the very pattern of kingdom entry; and wealth, which grips a sincere seeker so tightly that he turns away from eternal life. Together they show what it means, and what it costs, to receive the kingdom.
section.thesis: The kingdom of God is entered not by achievement or possession but received like a child, so that God's design for marriage is honoured, the lowly are welcomed, and even a moral, wealthy seeker must let go of what he trusts, for what is impossible with man is possible with God.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the teaching on divorce and God's design for marriage; the blessing of the children as the model of receiving the kingdom; and the rich man who cannot let go, with the promise to those who leave all for Jesus.

verses.text: Then Jesus left that place and went into the region of Judea, beyond the Jordan. Again the crowds came to Him and He taught them, as was His custom.
Some Pharisees came to test Him. “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?” they inquired.
“What did Moses command you?” He replied.
They answered, “Moses permitted a man to write his wife a certificate of divorce and send her away.”
But Jesus told them, “Moses wrote this commandment for you because of your {{sklerokardia|hardness of heart}}.
However, from the beginning of creation, ‘God made them male and female.’
‘For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife,
and the two will become one flesh.’ So they are no longer two, but one flesh.
Therefore what God has joined together, let man not separate.”
When they were back inside the house, the disciples asked Jesus about this matter.
So He told them, “Whoever {{apolyo|divorces}} his wife and marries another woman commits adultery against her.
And if a woman divorces her husband and marries another man, she commits adultery.”

units.label: Jesus blesses the children
units.note: receive the kingdom like a little child

verses.text: Now people were bringing the little children to Jesus for Him to place His hands on them, and the disciples rebuked those who brought them.
But when Jesus saw this, He was indignant and told them, “Let the little {{paidion|children}} come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of God belongs to such as these.
Truly I tell you, anyone who does not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it.”
And He took the children in His arms, placed His hands on them, and blessed them.

units.label: The rich man who could not let go

verses.text: As Jesus started on His way, a man ran up and knelt before Him. “Good Teacher,” he asked, “what must I do to inherit {{aionios|eternal}} life?”
“Why do you call Me {{agathos|good}}?” Jesus replied. “No one is good except God alone.
You know the commandments: ‘Do not murder, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not bear false witness, do not cheat others, honor your father and mother.’”
“Teacher,” he replied, “all these I have kept from my youth.”
Jesus looked at him, loved him, and said to him, “There is one thing you lack: Go, sell everything you own and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow Me.”
But the man was saddened by these words and went away in sorrow, because he had great wealth.
Then Jesus looked around and said to His disciples, “How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God!”
And the disciples were amazed at His words. But Jesus said to them again, “Children, how hard it is to enter the kingdom of God!
It is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.”
They were even more astonished and said to one another, “Who then can be saved?”
Jesus looked at them and said, “With man this is impossible, but not with God. For all things are possible with God.”
Peter began to say to Him, “Look, we have left everything and followed You.”
“Truly I tell you,” Jesus replied, “no one who has left home or brothers or sisters or mother or father or children or fields for My sake and for the gospel
will fail to receive a hundredfold in the present age, houses and brothers and sisters and mothers and children and fields, along with persecutions, and in the age to come, eternal life.
But many who are first will be last, and the last will be first.”

sklerokardia.gloss: Hardness of heart. Jesus says the Mosaic provision for divorce was a concession to human hardness, not God's original design. It names the stubborn, unyielding condition of the fallen heart that God's law regulated but the gospel comes to heal and renew.

apolyo.gloss: To release, send away, divorce. In a culture where a man could dismiss his wife with a certificate, Jesus reasserts the permanence of marriage grounded in creation, so that to divorce and remarry is to break the one-flesh union God has joined.

paidion.gloss: A young child, an infant. Jesus makes the child the pattern of kingdom entry: not because children are innocent achievers but because they receive with empty-handed dependence. The kingdom is welcomed, not earned, like a gift a small child simply receives.

aionios.gloss: Eternal, everlasting, belonging to the age to come. Eternal life is the life of God's kingdom, both a present gift and a future inheritance. The rich man asks how to inherit it, revealing that even the sincere and moral cannot secure it by their own doing.

agathos.gloss: Good. Jesus' question, why do you call Me good, no one is good except God alone, is not a denial of His own goodness but a probe: does the man realize that true goodness belongs to God, and that in calling Jesus good he speaks truer than he knows?

basic.context: As Jesus travels toward Jerusalem, Pharisees test Him on divorce. He points past the Mosaic concession to God's design at creation: one man and one woman joined as one flesh, not to be separated. Then people bring little children, and when the disciples try to shoo them away, Jesus is indignant, insisting the kingdom belongs to such as these and must be received like a child. Next a rich, moral man runs up asking how to inherit eternal life. Jesus, loving him, tells him to sell everything and follow, but he goes away sorrowful. Jesus warns how hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom, that only God can save, and promises abundant reward to all who leave everything for Him.
basic.hermeneutics: The three scenes all probe the heart's true trust. Marriage is grounded in God's creation order, not human convenience. The child models empty-handed dependence, the only way into the kingdom. The rich man shows that even sincere morality cannot save when the heart clings to wealth. The thread is that the kingdom is received as a gift, and receiving it may cost everything.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus traces divorce to sklerokardia, hardness of heart, against God's design; to apolyo, divorce, and remarry breaks the one-flesh union. The kingdom must be received like a paidion, a little child. A man asks how to inherit aionios, eternal, life, and calls Jesus agathos, good, truer than he knows.
basic.typology: God's design of marriage as one flesh points to the union of Christ and His church. The child received into Jesus' arms pictures the way every believer is welcomed. The rich man's failure highlights the treasure in heaven found only in following Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus is the authoritative interpreter of God's law and creation design, the one who welcomes children, and the treasure worth more than all wealth. He looks on the rich man and loves him, even as He calls him to costly discipleship.
basic.god: God designed marriage and joins husband and wife. God receives the lowly and dependent. God alone can save those whom wealth or self-reliance would exclude; with Him the impossible is possible.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1 and 2 ground marriage. Malachi 2 shows God's hatred of faithless divorce. Ephesians 5 unfolds marriage as a picture of Christ and the church.
basic.oneStory: On the way to the cross, Jesus reveals the heart of the kingdom: it cannot be earned, only received like a child. God's good design for marriage, His welcome of the lowly, and His call to let go of every rival treasure all point to the One who gave up everything for us, so that what is impossible for us is made possible by God.

context.literary: Chapter 10 continues the journey to Jerusalem, structured by teaching on discipleship. The divorce debate, the children, and the rich man form a triptych on entering the kingdom, followed by the third Passion prediction. The rich man's inability to leave his wealth contrasts sharply with the disciples who left everything, and with the child who has nothing to give.
context.historical: First century rabbis debated the grounds for divorce from Deuteronomy 24, some permitting it for almost any cause. Children had little social status, so the disciples' dismissal was culturally natural. The camel and the needle's eye was a proverb of impossibility. Great wealth was widely regarded as a sign of God's blessing, making Jesus' words about the rich shocking.
context.canonical: Jesus grounds marriage in Genesis 1:27 and 2:24, treating them as God's abiding creation ordinance, later expounded in Ephesians 5:31-32 as a picture of Christ and the church. Receiving the kingdom as a child echoes the Old Testament's blessing on the humble and dependent, Psalm 131. The call to treasure in heaven anticipates Jesus' teaching in Matthew 6 and the whole New Testament ethic of holding possessions loosely.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach that the kingdom of God is received as a gift, like a child, and to test the heart's trust through marriage, children, and wealth, showing that God's design must be honoured, the lowly welcomed, and every rival treasure surrendered, since salvation is impossible for man but possible with God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Jesus' teaching on God's design for marriage and on receiving the kingdom as a child is prescriptive and enduring. The specific command to the rich man to sell everything addresses that man's particular idol; it reveals the universal principle that nothing may rival Christ, though not every disciple is called to divest all property, as the reference to houses and fields shows.

debates.question: Is Jesus commanding every believer to sell all their possessions?

views.label: A diagnosis of this man's specific idol
views.case: Jesus, who elsewhere assumes disciples keep homes and help the poor from their means, exposes the one thing gripping this man's heart, his wealth; the command unmasks his true god, showing that following Christ must come first.

views.label: A radical call that may indeed require literal renunciation
views.case: For some, as for this man, discipleship really does demand giving up wealth; the principle that treasure in heaven outweighs earthly riches can require concrete, costly surrender, not merely an inner attitude.

debates.bearing: Both hold that Christ must be supreme over every possession; whether the surrender is literal or of the heart's grip, nothing may rival Him, and only God can free a clutching heart.

debates.question: Why does Jesus respond, why do you call Me good?

views.label: A probe toward His true identity
views.case: Jesus is not disowning goodness but pressing the man to see that if only God is good and Jesus is good, then Jesus is more than a mere teacher; the question invites a deeper recognition of who stands before him.

views.label: An exposure of shallow flattery and self-confidence
views.case: The man's easy compliment and claim to have kept the commandments reveal a superficial view of goodness; Jesus sets the true standard, God alone, to unsettle the man's confidence in his own record.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jesus redirects the man from casual moral self-assurance to the searching truth that goodness belongs to God, and that eternal life is God's gift, not a wage.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus traces the Mosaic divorce provision to sklerokardia, hardness of heart, a concession to human sin rather than God's design, and reasserts the creation pattern so that to apolyo, divorce, and remarry breaks the one-flesh union God has joined. He then makes the paidion, the little child, the very model of kingdom entry: the kingdom is received with a child's empty-handed dependence, not earned. When a man asks how to inherit aionios, eternal, life and calls Jesus agathos, good, Jesus turns the word back on him, since only God is good, inviting him to see whom he is really addressing and how far short his own goodness falls.

typology.fulfillment: The union of Christ and His church, the mystery marriage signifies, Genesis 2:24 and Ephesians 5:31-32.

typology.type: The child received into Jesus' arms
typology.fulfillment: Every believer welcomed by grace into the kingdom, received not for merit but for empty-handed trust, Psalm 131 and Matthew 18:3.

typology.type: Treasure in heaven over earthly wealth
typology.fulfillment: Christ Himself as the surpassing treasure worth losing all to gain, Matthew 13:44 and Philippians 3:8.

study.christ: Jesus speaks with the authority of the Creator, reaching behind Moses to God's original design for marriage, and He receives the children the world overlooks into His own arms. When the rich man kneels before Him, Jesus looks at him and loves him, and out of that love calls him to the one surrender he cannot make, exposing the idol of wealth. Jesus is Himself the treasure in heaven, worth more than everything the man owned, and the One who gave up all riches to make us rich. And His word that with God all things are possible points to the salvation He will accomplish, freeing clutching hearts that could never free themselves.
study.god: God is the author of marriage, who joins husband and wife as one flesh and intends the union to last. God delights to receive the lowly and dependent, welcoming little children as heirs of the kingdom while the proud and self-sufficient turn away sorrowful. And God alone can save: what is impossible for man, the freeing of a heart bound to wealth or self, is possible with Him. The whole passage magnifies a God whose kingdom is a gift to be received, not a prize to be earned, and whose grace reaches where human effort fails.

crossReferences.note: A man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and they will become one flesh, the creation ground of Jesus' teaching on marriage.
God's declared hatred of faithless divorce, underscoring the seriousness of the covenant Jesus defends.
Unless you become like little children you will never enter the kingdom, the same call to childlike reception.
Paul counts all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, the treasure the rich man could not grasp.

study.oneStory: On the road to the cross, Jesus lays bare the heart of the kingdom: it is received, not achieved, welcomed like a child with empty hands. He honours God's good design for marriage, embraces the lowly the world dismisses, and calls a sincere, wealthy seeker to surrender the treasure his heart clings to. The tragedy of the man who walks away sorrowful and the promise to those who leave all for Jesus point to the same truth: only God can save a clutching heart. And the One who calls us to let go is Himself the treasure worth more than all, the One who gave up everything to make us His.

quiz.q: How does Jesus answer the question about divorce?

options.text: He points past Moses' concession to God's creation design: one man and one woman joined as one flesh, not to be separated.
options.note: Mark 10:5-9; Jesus grounds marriage in creation, calling the Mosaic permission a concession to hard hearts.

options.text: He says Moses' certificate of divorce settles the matter.
options.note: Jesus says that provision was for hardness of heart, not God's original intent.

options.text: He upholds the permanence of marriage while acknowledging human sin.
options.note: True in substance, but it should name the creation ground: what God has joined, let no one separate.

options.text: He declares that marriage does not matter to God.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus stresses that God joins and honours marriage.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus say the kingdom must be received like a little child?

options.text: Because it is received with empty-handed dependence, as a gift, not earned by achievement.
options.note: Mark 10:15; entry is by childlike reception, not merit.

options.text: Because children are morally innocent and sinless.
options.note: The point is dependence and reception, not a claim of childhood innocence.

options.text: Because the humble and lowly are welcomed by God.
options.note: True and related, but the specific lesson is receiving the kingdom with a child's empty-handed trust.

options.text: Because children can earn God's favour more easily.
options.note: No one earns the kingdom; it is received as a gift, which is the whole point.

quiz.q: Why did the rich man go away sorrowful?

options.text: He would not surrender the wealth his heart clung to, though Jesus, loving him, called him to follow.
options.note: Mark 10:21-22; his great wealth held him back from following Jesus.

options.text: Because Jesus told him he had not kept the commandments.
options.note: The man claimed to have kept them; his failure was the idol of wealth Jesus exposed.

options.text: Because he was unwilling to part with what he trusted more than Christ.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should note Jesus' love for him and the specific call to sell all and follow.

options.text: Because Jesus refused to give him eternal life.
options.note: Jesus lovingly showed him the way; the man turned away himself.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean that with God the impossible is possible for salvation?

options.text: No one, rich or otherwise, can save themselves, but God can save even those bound by wealth or self-reliance.
options.note: Mark 10:27; salvation is God's work, possible with Him though impossible for man.

options.text: That rich people simply need to try harder to enter the kingdom.
options.note: The opposite; human effort cannot achieve it, which is why only God can save.

options.text: That God can rescue those whom human effort cannot save.
options.note: True, but it should include that this applies to everyone, since with man salvation is impossible.

options.text: That salvation is possible only for the poor.
options.note: The rich can be saved too, but only by God's power, not by their own doing.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise those who leave everything for Him and the gospel?

options.text: A hundredfold now, homes and family and fields along with persecutions, and eternal life in the age to come.
options.note: Mark 10:29-30 pairs abundant present reward, including persecutions, with future eternal life.

options.text: A hundredfold of homes, family, and fields in this present age.
options.note: True but partial; Jesus adds along with persecutions and eternal life in the age to come.

options.text: A trouble-free life of comfort and prosperity.
options.note: Jesus explicitly includes persecutions; the reward is not a comfortable life.

options.text: Nothing in this life, only reward after death.
options.note: Jesus promises a hundredfold in the present age as well as eternal life to come.

questions: Jesus rooted marriage in God's creation design rather than human convenience. How does seeing marriage as something God joins shape the way you view commitment and faithfulness?
Jesus welcomed the children the disciples tried to turn away. Who are the overlooked people you might be tempted to hinder from coming to Jesus?
The kingdom must be received like a child, with empty hands. Where are you still trying to earn what God gives only as a gift?
Jesus looked at the rich man and loved him, yet named the one thing he could not release. What is the one thing your heart most struggles to surrender to Christ?
Jesus promised abundant reward, with persecutions, to those who leave all for Him. How does that honest promise reshape what you expect from following Jesus?

translationNotes.note: And if a woman divorces her husband reflects a setting where, unlike strict Jewish custom, a wife might initiate divorce; Jesus applies the same standard to both, an unusually even-handed word for the time.
Some manuscripts add take up the cross after come, follow Me, echoing Mark 8:34; either way the call is to wholehearted, costly discipleship.
Some manuscripts read how hard it is for those who trust in riches to enter, interpreting Jesus' point: the peril is not wealth itself but trusting in it rather than in God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 10:1-31. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The two em dashes in verse 30 were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
